prompt:
Create a Meridian Wellness Travel & Clinic Booking platform that lets customers browse wellness travel products while reserving on-site clinician or coach sessions as part of their itinerary, all through an English React web interface designed for kiosk and office use without an internet connection. Travelers (regular users) can maintain a personal profile with multiple contacts and a frequent address book for billing and printed confirmations; the UI normalizes US addresses as the user types (standardizing abbreviations like “St.” vs “Street”), detects duplicates, and warns when an address falls outside configured service coverage for home pickup or mailed documents. Customers explore destinations, routes, partner hotels, and attractions with rich text descriptions, locally stored images, and publish/unpublish visibility; each package shows a pricing calendar by date with clear inventory remaining and blackout notes (for example, “no arrivals after 6:00 PM”). Booking flows present real-time bookable time slots for clinic rooms/chairs and assigned hosts based on selected service duration (e.g., 30/45/60 minutes) and business hours, with immediate feedback when a slot becomes unavailable during checkout. A built-in community layer supports Q&A and threaded comments on trips and providers, with likes, favorites, follows, reporting, and blocking; in-app notifications inform users of replies, status changes, and moderation outcomes, while “email” is limited to an internal, extensible template queue that can be exported for manual sending in offline operations. Administrators and staff use analytics screens to review KPIs such as booking volume, attendance rate, repurchase rate, refund rate, and coach utilization, with filters by date range, provider, and package, plus one-click exports and scheduled report generation saved as local files.
 On the system side, the backend uses Go with Echo to expose REST-style APIs consumed by the React frontend over the local network, keeping a decoupled architecture while remaining fully offline-runnable. PostgreSQL persists all operational data, including customer master records, regional hierarchies, service rules (deliverable time windows, restricted regions, and allow/block lists), travel catalog entities (destinations, routes, hotels, attractions, pricing calendars, quotas), community content, and reporting snapshots. Authentication is local username/password only; passwords must be at least 12 characters with complexity checks, stored using a strong salted hash, and accounts lock for 15 minutes after 5 failed attempts. RBAC supports roles aligned to student/teacher/admin concepts (traveler, coach/clinician, operations staff, administrator) with menu, API, and data-scope permissions; permission changes are audited with who/when/what, and sensitive fields (phone numbers, street addresses, medical-consult notes if captured) are masked in UI and encrypted at rest using application-level encryption keys stored on the server host. Scheduling models support multi-room and multi-chair resources, recurring availability, exception days, and holidays; slot granularity is configurable (default 15 minutes), and bookable slots are generated dynamically per host, room, and script duration. During order placement, the system performs multi-dimensional conflict validation (host, room/chair, and inventory quota) and prevents overselling via optimistic locking with version checks and a short-lived reservation hold (e.g., 10 minutes) that auto-releases if not confirmed, ensuring consistent outcomes even with concurrent staff terminals.
